Modeling by [Korinek and Suh](https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w32255/w32255.pdf#page=25) demonstrates that by default, wages plummet. Expanding the social safety net will be limited by several constraints. Fiscal constraints worldwide are tight due to rising rates and debts[^5]. [Economic modelling](https://inferencemagazine.substack.com/p/how-much-economic-growth-from-ai) suggests that the truly explosive growth rates needed to fund widespread UBI will only arrive when we get general-purpose robotics. There are also many cultural considerations: many high-earning households may not be satisfied with earning the same UBI as everyone else, and losing the track to ambition they dreamed of, especially if they're early in their careers.
response_rejected: Frankly stated, the conventional white-collar jobs that drive developed economies are not expected to withstand the impact of the AI revolution. This issue is not a distant concern for 2050 or 2100 but an immediate challenge for current business leaders, government officials, and societal structures. While it's commonly suggested that AI will lead to the creation of new, improved employment opportunities and higher pay, economists who seriously consider AI's implications tend to disagree. For instance, models developed by Korinek and Suh indicate a standard outcome of sharply falling wages. Efforts to broaden social welfare systems face significant hurdles. Globally, governments are under financial pressure from increasing interest rates and national debts. Economic analyses also propose that the substantial economic expansion required to support universal basic income (UBI) is unlikely to materialize until the advent of general-purpose robotics. response_accepted:
1. LLMs are products of amortised optimisation, and most of their strength does lie in doing tasks that are more like amortised optimisation: distilling and synthesising information from their past "experience", and being extremely knowledgeable. LLMs already play the role of a wise guru to many: any programmer these days will use LLMs like they might have used an especially-available senior programmer who knows the best established practice for every scenario. It is true that they seem worse at having useful world models than humans are, and their wisdom runs less deep. More than that, perhaps, their ability to seem wise is dented by their blandness, a result both of their pre-training to mimic average internet text, and the fine-tuning done on them. They also seem quite bad at epistemics; as discussed in the first part, some kind of good epistemics is often (but not always) required to be able to make good use of the products of amortised optimisation, even if you have them at hand. Currently, that has to be largely provided by the human who's interacting with the LLM.
2. The extent to which LLMs do direct optimisation within a forward pass is unclear. However, like policy and value networks in MCTS, LLMs can be used as part of a direct optimisation process. Most trivially, you can prompt an LLM to search over some action space, and then evaluate the actions and pick one. LLM scaffolds go much further. It is true that the amortised optimisation core is still doing a lot of the work there, but that is true also of the amortised optimisation parts of MCTS variants that use neural networks, and probably of human thinking too. Therefore, LLMs can clearly be used for direct optimisation as well. In particular, we should expect LLMs to increase that part of the world's direct optimisation where the quality of the outputs is not hard to judge - lots of automating boilerplate writing and maths, less automating business decisions and philosophy.
response_rejected:
Large Language Models (LLMs) primarily derive their capabilities from amortised optimisation, meaning they excel at tasks involving the distillation and synthesis of previously encountered information, making them highly knowledgeable. Many professionals, such as programmers, already utilize LLMs as readily available experts, similar to how one might consult a senior colleague for established best practices. However, LLMs appear to possess less sophisticated world models compared to humans, and their wisdom seems more superficial. Their perceived wisdom is further diminished by a certain generic quality, stemming from their training on broad internet data and subsequent fine-tuning. Additionally, they demonstrate weaknesses in epistemic reasoning; effective use of amortised optimisation often requires strong epistemics, which, for now, must typically be supplied by the human user.
Regarding direct optimisation during their operation, the degree to which LLMs engage in this is not fully understood. Nevertheless, LLMs can be integrated into direct optimisation procedures, much like policy and value networks in Monte Carlo Tree Search. For instance, an LLM can be instructed to explore a range of possible actions, which are then assessed to select the best option. More complex systems, known as LLM scaffolds, extend this capability. While the underlying amortised optimisation still performs a significant role, this is also characteristic of neural network-based MCTS variations and likely human cognition. Consequently, LLMs are demonstrably capable of contributing to direct optimisation, particularly in areas where output quality is easily assessable, such as automating routine coding and mathematical tasks, rather than complex business or philosophical decision-making.

response_accepted:
In the ancestral environment and still today, a key driver of cultural evolution is [prestige-biased social learning](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-019-0228-7): people imitating the habits of people who seem successful. Successful here can mean either directly successful, like a tendency to successfully get fresh meat or build companies, or just high in perceived status because other people pay attention to them (modern celebrities are a side product of this instinct). That humans can do this seems to be a big part of the human cognitive advantage compared to other apes. It's clear that this is a big boost to cultural evolution: unlike genes, memes don't have to rely on the differential survival of their hosts to spread, since nearby hosts will tend to copy the meme the instant the meme's host seems to be doing well.
None of this requires understanding why the memes work, or even which memes are driving someone's success. In Fiji, there are [taboos](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2992713/) against pregnant women eating sharks. The reason this is useful is that the sharks contain chemicals that increase birth defect risks, but no one knew this. This is an insanely sophisticated and subtle meme: the causal connection between the sharks and the birth defects is long, weak, and totally beyond understanding before modern science.
There's a story that the famous mathematician Paul Erdos used to touch walls as he walked, and other mathematicians started imitating this behaviour - maybe it was the secret to Erdos's success, after all. Given our species' history, this isn't as crazy as it seems.
response_rejected:
A fundamental mechanism in human cultural development, both historically and currently, is prestige-biased social learning, where individuals copy the behaviors of those perceived as successful. Success can be direct, such as proficiency in acquiring resources or establishing enterprises, or it can be based on perceived status due to public attention, as seen with modern celebrities. This human capacity for imitation is a significant factor in our cognitive superiority over other primates and greatly accelerates cultural evolution. Unlike genetic traits, cultural ideas (memes) can proliferate rapidly as individuals adopt them from seemingly thriving peers, without needing to wait for the slower process of differential survival.
This imitation often occurs without any comprehension of why certain behaviors are effective or which specific actions contribute to success. For example, Fijian cultural taboos prohibit pregnant women from consuming sharks. This practice is beneficial because sharks contain substances that elevate the risk of birth defects, a fact unknown to the originators of the taboo. This represents a remarkably complex and nuanced cultural adaptation, as the link between shark consumption and birth defects is indirect, subtle, and was unexplainable before the advent of modern science.
An anecdote illustrates this tendency: the renowned mathematician Paul Erdos reportedly had a habit of touching walls while walking, a quirk that some fellow mathematicians began to emulate, perhaps speculating it was linked to his genius. Considering the history of human social learning, such imitative behavior is not as irrational as it might initially appear.

response_accepted: In contrast, the AIs themselves might be very good at cultural evolution. Whether or not AI *learning* will be more or less sample-efficient than human learning is still unclear, so they may continue to require more examples to learn something than humans. However, AIs can be copied once they've learnt something. The best AI at a task can instantly become most of the entire global AI population working on that task (depending on the surrounding political, economic, and business environment, this may be by its own choice, the choice of another AI, or a human's choice). Much like human cultures develop memes that no individual could've come up with, the AIs might develop adaptations that they themselves could not have come up with, not through any direct optimisation that they're doing, or even through explicit training, but through external selection pressures and selective mimicry in the population of AIs. If humans took over the world by dint of cultural evolution despite individual human capabilities being static, imagine what ever-improving AIs might do.

Human cultural evolution, though far more rapid than genetic change, is still a relatively slow process: it necessitates an individual demonstrating a successful behavior, others recognizing this success, and then those individuals learning to replicate the behavior, which can involve extensive practice. It remains uncertain whether AI learning will ultimately be more or less efficient than human learning in terms of the data required, so AIs might still need more examples than humans to acquire new skills. A key difference, however, is that once an AI has learned something, it can be duplicated almost instantaneously. The rapid dissemination of LLM prompts and frameworks like LLM scaffolds exemplifies this; envision this type of selective force operating over extended periods on AIs with progressively enhancing individual abilities. Similar to how human societies develop cultural innovations beyond the capacity of any single person, AIs could evolve adaptations they couldn't have devised on their own—not through their own direct problem-solving or explicit programming, but via external selective forces and mimicry within the AI population. Given that human dominance was achieved through cultural evolution even with static individual human abilities, one can only speculate on the potential of continuously improving AIs.

A more serious harm is that LLM text might be skewed to be more like median human text than human text is, as argued [here](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.03502). This might reduce richness and variety in areas where LLM text replaces human text - i.e., eventually probably everything. In the same way that industrialisation made many physical products cheaper and better, but also more uniform and less personalised, AI might do the same for products of mental work. This will likely be worse than the loss of personalisation with physical goods, since diversity and variety is more of the value of mental work, and therefore people will pay a steeper premium to maintain it than they would in physical products. Less variety would also mean a smaller population of memes for cultural evolution to select from. It also seems to be harder for LLMs to learn from LLM text - see for example [this paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.17493), or [this exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.07043) of how scaling laws change when there's LLM data in the mix. However, this type of "model collapse" shouldn't be exaggerated - it seems that dramatic slowdowns on LLM progress are [not likely from it](https://x.com/RylanSchaeffer/status/1816881533795422404).
A big problem with current human written knowledge is that it often can't be used effectively. There isn't enough time to read everything, or find every useful paper on a topic. Lots of knowledge, rather than being part of amortised optimisation processes, sits ignored. LLMs could help. Already, LLMs are doing a good job as forecasters simply by having the patience to read every news article, or at [finding papers on a topic](https://elicit.com/), or [getting close to beating Google](https://www.perplexity.ai/). The ultimate search method is talking to a wise guru who knows everything; LLMs are tending towards this.
LLMs could also help reduce ["research debt"](https://distill.pub/2017/research-debt/), the mountain of poor explanations, undigested thoughts, and noise that researchers have to process to get to the frontier. They're already decent at answering simple questions about a paper. In the future, they might distil a set of papers into a [Chris Olah -level explanation](https://colah.github.io/posts/2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs/) that could be used by humans or AIs. This would be a very good situation for humans - think of how fun good explanations are to read - but will human mental labour still be relevant once this is possible? Producing a good explanation sounds close to an AGI-complete problem, but if AI disproportionately improves at amortised optimisation without getting much better at direct optimisation, a world where AIs can explain existing things well but humans are still needed for novel discoveries may exist for a while.

A significant concern is that content generated by LLMs might tend towards an average or 'median' version of human writing, potentially diminishing the diversity and richness in fields where AI-generated text becomes dominant—which could eventually encompass nearly all areas. Similar to how industrial manufacturing led to more affordable and improved physical goods but also greater uniformity and less customization, AI could have a comparable effect on intellectual creations. This issue might be more acute for mental work than for physical products because the value of intellectual output heavily relies on its diversity and originality, meaning people might be willing to pay more to preserve these qualities. A reduction in variety would also shrink the pool of cultural ideas available for evolutionary selection. Furthermore, there's evidence suggesting that LLMs find it more challenging to learn effectively from text generated by other LLMs, a phenomenon termed 'model collapse,' although the extent to which this will impede LLM advancement is debatable and possibly overstated.
A major challenge with existing human-generated written knowledge is its underutilization. The sheer volume of information makes it impossible to read everything or locate every relevant publication on a given subject. Consequently, a vast amount of knowledge remains dormant instead of contributing to collective learning processes. LLMs offer a potential solution here. They are already proving effective in tasks like forecasting, due to their capacity to process extensive information like all news articles, and in academic research by aiding in paper discovery or providing comprehensive answers that rival search engines. The ideal information retrieval system would be like consulting an omniscient sage, and LLMs are moving in that direction.
Additionally, LLMs could alleviate 'research debt'—the accumulation of unclear explanations, unrefined ideas, and general clutter that researchers must navigate to reach the cutting edge of their fields. Current LLMs can already provide reasonable answers to straightforward questions about academic papers. In the future, they might be capable of synthesizing multiple papers into exceptionally clear explanations, akin to those by renowned communicators like Chris Olah, benefiting both humans and AIs. While this would be highly advantageous for human understanding—as high-quality explanations are enjoyable to engage with—it raises questions about the continued relevance of human intellectual effort. Creating such explanations appears to be a task nearing AGI-level complexity. However, if AI's progress in amortised optimisation significantly outpaces its development in direct optimisation, there might be a period where AIs excel at elucidating existing knowledge while humans remain essential for making new discoveries.

response_accepted:
In addition to being bad at strategy, another error of insufficient wisdom is having the wrong frame / ontology / paradigm. This is when you notice the Earth isn't the centre of the universe, or that a watch [doesn't](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darwin%27s_Dangerous_Idea#Skyhooks_and_cranes) imply a watchmaker. It's when you go to your wise mountain guru to ask how to finally find that treasure, and they tell you the real treasure is the friends you made along the way.
If there were a simple explanation for how paradigm shifts are found, many famous philosophers of science would be a lot less famous. However, a paradigm shift takes at least two things: someone has to discover the new framing, and many someones have to care.
Current LLMs are bad at anything like finding paradigm shifts. Consider how many people use LLMs, and the lack of any breakthrough, even of the size of a decent research paper, where LLMs were claimed to be the main source of the concept. And paradigm shifts are much harder than research papers.
Inventing important moral principles, like utilitarianism or the categorical imperative, seems even harder. Bentham's and Kant's search for moral principles was presumably guided by trying to make precise their intuitive human ethics. There's some amount of data with which they could've succeeded without having those built-in moral intuitions, but it seems very helpful to have had those intuitions in their head as something they could easily query.
It's in having people care that AI might have a larger effect, and maybe a bad one. First, one driver of paradigm shifts is that someone gets annoyed by complexity or ugliness, and wants to fix it. By making mental labour cheaper, AI might reduce the ickiness of bad models. Consider how geocentrism may have lasted longer if it were trivial to ask your Jupyter notebook copilot AI to add a few more epicycles to your model, and it didn't mean more laborious longhand arithmetic for you. Second, increasing use of AI might mean that less and less of the paradigm-incompatible data is seen by human eyes. Imagine if the AI adds the epicycles in the background to improve the model, without any human ever noticing. Potential fixes might be keeping simplicity and elegance as key cultural values in scientific fields, and somehow making propagating this to the AIs (while it has [many problems](https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/contra-the-xai-alignment-plan), xAI's "curious AIs" plan has some of this spirit).
response_rejected:
Beyond flawed strategic thinking, another mark of insufficient wisdom is adopting an incorrect framework, worldview, or conceptual model. This is akin to realizing that the Earth is not the universe's center, or that a complex design like a watch does not necessitate a deliberate designer, or, metaphorically, when a sage advises that the journey's true value lies in companionship rather than a sought-after prize.
The process of discovering paradigm shifts is complex; if it were straightforward, many renowned philosophers of science would not have achieved their fame. Nevertheless, a paradigm shift generally requires two key elements: the initial discovery of the new perspective by an individual, and subsequent widespread acceptance or concern by many others.
Contemporary LLMs are not proficient at tasks resembling the discovery of paradigm shifts. Despite their widespread use, there have been no significant breakthroughs, not even at the level of a notable research paper, attributed primarily to LLMs. Discovering new paradigms is a far more challenging endeavor than producing standard research.
The formulation of significant moral concepts, such as utilitarianism or the categorical imperative, appears to be an even more formidable task. Philosophers like Bentham and Kant, in their quest for moral frameworks, were likely guided by an effort to articulate their inherent human ethical intuitions. While they might have achieved some success with sufficient data alone, having these internal moral compasses readily available for consultation seems to have been highly beneficial.
AI's more substantial, and potentially detrimental, impact might be on the societal adoption of new paradigms. Firstly, a catalyst for paradigm shifts is often frustration with the complexity or inelegance of existing models, motivating a search for better alternatives. By reducing the effort involved in mental tasks, AI could diminish the perceived awkwardness of flawed models. For instance, the geocentric model of the universe might have persisted longer if astronomers could have effortlessly instructed an AI assistant to incorporate additional epicycles, thereby avoiding tedious manual calculations. Secondly, the increasing reliance on AI could lead to humans being exposed to less data that contradicts established paradigms. If an AI, for example, automatically refines a model by adding complexities like epicycles without human oversight or awareness, crucial anomalies might go unnoticed. Possible countermeasures include upholding simplicity and elegance as core values in scientific endeavors and finding ways to instill these values in AIs, a concept somewhat reflected in xAI's 'curious AIs' initiative, despite its own set of challenges.

response_accepted: If human civilisation is destroyed this century, the most likely cause is advanced AI systems. This might sound like a bold claim to many, given that we live on a planet full of existing concrete threats like climate change, over ten thousand nuclear weapons, and Vladimir Putin However, it is a conclusion that many people who think about the topic keep coming to. The “learning” part in “machine learning” makes it sound like something refined and sensible. This is a false impression. ML systems learn by going through a training process that looks like this:

**Step 1:** you define a statistical model. This takes the form of some equation that has some unknown constants (“parameters”) in it, and some variables where you plug in input values. Together, the parameters and input variables define an output. (The equations in ML can be _extremely_ large, for example with billions of parameters and millions of inputs, but they are very structured and almost stupidly simple.)

**Step 2** : you don’t know what parameters to put in the equation, but you can literally roll some dice if you want (or the computer equivalent).

**Step 3** : presumably there’s some task you want the ML system to do. Let it try. It will fail horribly and produce gibberish (c.f. the previous part where we just put random numbers everywhere).

**Step 4** : There's a simple algorithm called gradient descent, which, when using another algorithm called backpropagation to calculate the gradient, can tell you which direction all the parameters should be shifted to make the ML system slightly better (as judged, for example, by its performance on examples in a dataset).

**Step 5** : You shift all the numbers a bit based on the algorithm in step 4.

**Step 6** : Go back to step 3 (letting the system try). Repeat until (a) the system has stopped improving for a long time, (b) you get impatient, or - increasingly plausible these days - (c) you run out of your compute budget.
response_rejected: Contrary to the sophisticated image evoked by "machine learning," the process is somewhat cruder. It involves several key stages: First, a statistical model is established, essentially a large, structured but simple equation with adjustable parameters and inputs that determine an output. Second, these parameters are initially set, often randomly. Third, the system attempts its designated task, typically performing very poorly at first. Fourth, using techniques like gradient descent and backpropagation, the system identifies how to adjust its parameters for slight performance improvement on a given dataset. Fifth, these adjustments are made. Finally, this cycle of trial and adjustment (steps 3-5) is repeated until the system plateaus in improvement, the operator loses patience, or computational resources are exhausted.

One of the most common objections to risks from AI goes something like this:

> _If the AI is smart enough to cause a global catastrophe, isn’t it smart enough to know that this isn’t what humans wanted?_

The problem with this is that it conflates two different concepts: **intelligence** (in the sense of having the ability to achieve your goals, whatever they might be) and **having goals which are morally good by human standards**. When we look at humans, these two often go hand-in-hand. But the key observation of the orthogonality thesis is that this doesn’t have to be the case for all possible mind designs. As defined by Nick Bostrom in his book _[Superintelligence](https://nickbostrom.com/superintelligentwill.pdf)_ :

> **The Orthogonality Thesis**
> 
>  _Intelligence and final goals are orthogonal axes along which possible agents can freely vary. In other words, more or less any level of intelligence could in principle be combined with more or less any final goal._

Here, orthogonal means “at right angles” or “unrelated” – in other words we can imagine a graph with one axis representing intelligence, and another representing the agent’s goals, with any point in the graph representing a theoretically possible agent*. The classic example here is a **“paperclip maximiser”** \- a powerful AGI driven only by the goal of making paperclips.

While these qualities often coexist in humans, the orthogonality thesis posits that they are not inherently linked in all potential intelligent systems. Nick Bostrom's "Orthogonality Thesis" states that an agent's level of intelligence and its ultimate objectives can vary independently; any degree of intelligence could theoretically be paired with almost any conceivable goal. The term "orthogonal" signifies this independence, suggesting that intelligence and goals can be thought of as separate dimensions. A well-known illustration of this is the "paperclip maximiser," a hypothetical advanced AI singularly focused on producing paperclips.

Outer misalignment is perhaps the simpler concept to understand, because we encounter it all the time in everyday life, in a form called **Goodhart’s law**. In its most well-known form, this law states:

> _When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure._

Perhaps the most famous case comes from Soviet nail factories, which produced nails based on targets that they had been given by the central government. When a factory was given targets based on the total _number_ of nails produced, they ended up producing a massive number of tiny nails which couldn’t function properly. On the other hand, when the targets were based on the total _weight_ produced, the nails would end up huge and bulky, and equally impractical.

A more recent example comes from the COVID-19 pandemic, where a plasma donation centre offered COVID-sufferers a larger cash reward than healthy individuals. As a result, people would deliberately infect themselves with COVID-19 in order to get a larger cash reward. Examples like this could fill up an entire book, but hopefully at this point you get the message!

The capacity $C$ of a noisy channel is defined as
$$
C = \max_{p_x} I(X;Y) = \max_{p_x} \big(H(Y) - H(Y|X)\big).
$$
It's intuitive that this definition involves the mutual information $I$ (see [the first post for the definition and explanation](https://www.strataoftheworld.com/2022/06/information-theory-1.html)), since we care about how much information $X$ transfers to $Y$, and how much $Y$ tells us about $X$. What might be less obvious is why we take the maximum over possible input probability distributions $p_x$. This is because the mutual information $I(X;Y)$ depends on the probability distributions of $X$ and $Y$.  We can only control what we send - $X$ - so we want to adjust that to maximise the mutual information. Intuitively, if you're typing on a keyboard with all keys working normally except the "i" key results in a random character being inserted, shifting your typing away from using the "i" key is good for information transfer. Better to wr1te l1ke th1s than to not be able to reliably transfer information.

However, the only real way to understand why this definition makes sense is to look at the noisy channel coding theorem. This theorem tells us, among other things, that for any rate (measured in bits per symbol) smaller than the capacity $C$, for a large enough code length we can get a probability of error as small as we like.

The concept of a noisy channel's capacity, denoted $C$, is given by the formula $C = \max_{p_x} I(X;Y) = \max_{p_x} \big(H(Y) - H(Y|X)\big)$. It makes sense that mutual information ($I$) is part of this definition because it quantifies the information shared between the input ($X$) and output ($Y$). The reason for maximizing over all potential input distributions ($p_x$) might not be immediately clear. This is done because the mutual information is affected by the probabilities of $X$ and $Y$. Since we can only determine the input $X$, we adjust its distribution to get the highest possible mutual information. For instance, if a keyboard's "i" key produced random characters while others worked fine, avoiding the "i" key would improve how much information is successfully transmitted. It's more effective to type with deliberate substitutions (e.g., "wr1te l1ke th1s") than to risk unreliable communication.

Nonetheless, the noisy channel coding theorem provides the best justification for this definition. A key takeaway from this theorem is that for any transmission rate (in bits per symbol) below the channel capacity $C$, it's possible to achieve an arbitrarily low error probability by using a sufficiently long code.

Before we look at the noisy channel theorem, here's a simple code that is redundant to error: transmit every bit 3 times. Instead of sending 010, send 000111000. If the receiver receives 010111000, they can tell that bit 2 probably had an error, and should be a zero. The problem is that you triple your message length.

Hamming codes are a method for achieving the same - the ability to detect and correct single-bit errors, and the ability to detect but not properly correct two-bit errors - while sending a number of excess bits that grows only logarithmically with message length. For long enough messages, this is very efficient; if you're sending over 250 bits, it only costs you a 3% longer message to insure them against single-bit errors.

The catch is that the probability of having only one or fewer errors in a message declines exponentially with message length, so this is less impressive than it might sound at first.

In contrast, Hamming codes offer a way to achieve similar error management capabilities—specifically, correcting single-bit errors and detecting (but not fully correcting) two-bit errors—by adding a number of extra bits that increases logarithmically with the message's length. This approach is highly efficient for longer messages; for instance, protecting a message over 250 bits long against single-bit errors increases its length by only about 3%.

However, there's a caveat: the likelihood of a message experiencing one error or fewer diminishes exponentially as the message length increases. Consequently, this efficiency might not be as remarkable as it initially seems.

We know that it's initial battery level was $b_0$, and the battery level when it was at each of $Y_1$, $Y_2$, and $Y_f$ is $B_1$, $B_2$, and $B_f$, where each of those is generated from the previous and the heading/velocity parameters $h_1$, $h_2$, and $h_f$ (e.g. $B_2 = B_1  - (1 + \epsilon) |h_1|$ – the amount of power lost is a normal error term plus a constant times the velocity). We need to find the probability that the next battery level $B_n$, a random variable generated from $B_f$ (the previous level) and depending on $Y_f$ (since storm intensity varies with position; say we have a function $s$ that takes in positions and returns how much the dust will decrease power output and hence batter level at a particular position, then we might have $B_n = B_f - s(Y_f)$), is below a critical threshold $c$, given the starting $X$, and the position readings $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_f$. Also the administrator of NASA is breathing down your neck because this is a 2 billion dollar mission, so better work fast and not make mistakes.

This problem seems almost intractably complicated. A handy way of making complex probability questions less unapproachable is to draw out a causal diagram: what are the key parameters, and which random variables are generated from which other ones?

AI alignment is a strange field. Unlike other fields which study potential risks to the future of humanity (e.g. nuclear war or climate change), there is almost no precedent for the kinds of risks we care about. Additionally, because of the nature of the threat, failing to get alignment right on the first try might be fatal. As Paul Christiano (a well-known AI safety researcher) recently wrote:

> _Humanity usually solves technical problems by  **iterating and fixing failures** ; we often resolve tough methodological disagreements very slowly by seeing what actually works and having our failures thrown in our face. But it will probably be possible to build valuable AI products without solving alignment, and so **reality won’t “force us” to solve alignment until it’s too late**. This seems like a case where we will have to be **unusually reliant on careful reasoning rather than empirical feedback loops** for some of the highest-level questions._

For these reasons, the field of AI alignment lacks a consensus on how the problem should be tackled, or what the most important parts of the problem even are. This is why there is a lot of variety in the approaches we present in this post.
response_rejected: The discipline of AI alignment is quite unusual. Compared to other areas addressing potential human future threats, such as nuclear war or climate change, the specific types of risks associated with AI are largely without historical parallel. Moreover, given the critical nature of this threat, an initial failure in achieving proper AI alignment could prove catastrophic. Paul Christiano, a noted AI safety researcher, observed that humans typically resolve technological issues through repeated attempts and learning from errors; significant disagreements about methods are often settled gradually by observing what is effective and confronting failures. However, it is likely feasible to create useful AI technologies without first addressing alignment, meaning that real-world pressures won't compel a solution until a crisis point is reached. This suggests that, for the most fundamental questions, AI alignment will need to depend more on careful, logical analysis than on learning from empirical outcomes. Consequently, there is no general agreement in the AI alignment field regarding the best strategies or even the most critical components of the problem, which accounts for the wide array of approaches discussed here.

“Iterated Distillation and Amplification” (IDA) is an imposing name, but the core intuition is simple. One of the ways in which an individual human can achieve more things is by delegating tasks to others. In turn, the assistants that tasks are delegated to can be expected to become more competent at the task.

In IDA, an AI plays the role of the assistant. “Distillation” refers to the abilities of the human being “distilled” into the AI through training, and “amplification” refers to the human becoming more capable as they can call on more and more powerful AI assistants to help them.

A setup to train an IDA personal assistant might go like this:

  1. You have a human, say Hannah, who knows how to carry out the tasks of a personal assistant.

  2. You have an ML model - call it Martin - that starts out knowing very little (perhaps nothing at all, or perhaps it’s a pre-trained language model so it knows how to read and write English but not much else).

  3. Hannah needs to find the answer to some questions, and she can invoke multiple copies of Martin to help her. Since Martin is quite useless at this stage, Hannah has to do even simple tasks herself, like writing routine emails. Using some interface legible to Martin, she breaks the email-writing task into subtasks like “find email address of Hu M. Anderson”, “select greeting”, “check project status”, “mention project status”, and so on.

  4. From seeing enough examples of Hannah’s own answers to the sub-questions, Martin’s training loop gradually trains it to be able to answer first the simpler sub-tasks - (address is “humanderson@humanmail.com”, greeting is “Salutations, Human Colleague!”, etc.) and eventually all the sub-tasks involved in routine email-writing.

  5. At this point, “write a routine email” becomes a task Martin can entirely carry out for Hannah. This is now a building block that can be used as a subtask in broader tasks Hannah gives out to Martin.  Once enough tasks become tasks that Martin can carry out by itself, Hannah can draft much larger goals, like “invade France”, and let Martin take care of details like “blackmail Emmanuel Macron”, “write battle plan for the French Alps”, and “select a suitable coronation dress”.
response_rejected:
Despite its somewhat formidable name, "Iterated Distillation and Amplification" (IDA) rests on a straightforward idea. Humans often accomplish more by assigning tasks to others, who, in doing so, typically become more proficient. In the IDA model, an AI serves as this assistant. "Distillation" describes the transfer of human capabilities to the AI through training, while "amplification" refers to the human's enhanced capacity resulting from the use of increasingly skilled AI aides.

An illustrative training process for an IDA personal assistant could unfold as follows: Consider Hannah, a human proficient in personal assistant duties, and Martin, a machine learning model with initially limited knowledge (perhaps only basic language skills if pre-trained). When Hannah requires information, she can utilize multiple instances of Martin. Initially, Martin is not very helpful, so Hannah performs simple tasks like email composition herself. She breaks down these tasks into smaller steps comprehensible to Martin, such as "locate Hu M. Anderson's email," "choose a salutation," or "verify project status." By observing Hannah's solutions to these sub-problems repeatedly, Martin's training process enables it to gradually learn, starting with the easier sub-tasks (like finding an email or selecting a greeting) and eventually mastering all components of routine email writing. Once Martin can independently compose a routine email, this skill becomes a foundational element for more complex tasks assigned by Hannah. As Martin becomes capable of autonomously handling more tasks, Hannah can delegate larger objectives, such as "plan the invasion of France," with Martin managing details like "blackmail Emmanuel Macron" or "choose an appropriate coronation gown."

Imagine you’re a bit drunk, but (as one does) you’re at a bar talking about AI alignment proposals. Someone’s talking about how even if you can get an advanced AI system to explain its reasoning to you, it might try to slip something very subtle past you and you might not notice. You might well blurt out: “well then just make it fight another AI over it!”

The OpenAI safety team presumably spends a fair amount of time at bars, because they’ve [investigated the idea of achieving safe AI by having two AIs debate each other](https://openai.com/blog/debate/) to persuade a panel of human judges, by trying to poke holes in each other’s arguments. For more complex tasks, the AIs could be given transparency tools deriving from interpretability research (see next section) that they can use on each other. Just like a Go-playing AI gets an unambiguous win-loss signal from either winning or losing, a debating AI gets an unambiguous win-loss signal from winning or losing the debate:

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9782253a-8c1f-44fc-a104-15c63d871f1e_400x143.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9782253a-8c1f-44fc-a104-15c63d871f1e_400x143.png)

In addition, having the type of AI that is trained to give answers that are maximally insightful and persuasive to humans seems like the type of thing that might not be terrible. Consider how in court, a prosecutor and defendant biased in opposite directions are generally assumed to converge on the truth. Unless, of course, maximising persuasiveness to humans - over accuracy or helpfulness - is exactly the type of thing that gets the worst parts of Goodhart’s law delivered to you by 24/7 Amazon Prime express delivery.
response_rejected:
Picture a casual discussion about AI alignment proposals, perhaps in an informal setting, where someone raises a concern: even if an advanced AI explains its reasoning, it might subtly deceive you without your knowledge. A spontaneous suggestion might be to make it debate another AI.

The OpenAI safety team has explored this very notion—achieving AI safety by having two AIs engage in a debate to convince human judges, with each AI attempting to identify weaknesses in the other's arguments. For more complicated tasks, these AIs could be equipped with transparency tools from interpretability research to analyze each other. Similar to how a Go-playing AI receives clear feedback from a win or loss, a debating AI would get a definite signal from winning or losing the debate.

Moreover, developing AI systems trained to provide responses that are highly insightful and persuasive to humans seems like a potentially positive direction. This is comparable to a courtroom scenario where a prosecutor and a defense attorney, despite their opposing biases, are generally expected to help reveal the truth. The caveat, however, is that prioritizing persuasiveness to humans over accuracy or helpfulness could lead to the most problematic aspects of Goodhart's law being rapidly and efficiently realized.

response_accepted:
If you look at fundamental problems with current ML systems, #1 is probably something like this: in general we don’t have any idea what an ML model is doing, because it’s multiplying massive inscrutable matrices of floating-point numbers with other massive inscrutable matrices of floating point numbers, and it’s pretty hard to stare at that and answer questions about what the model is actually doing. Is it thinking hard about whether an image is a cat or a dog? Is it counting up electric sheep? Is it daydreaming about the AI revolution? Who knows!

If you had to figure out an answer to such a question today, your best bet might be to call Chris Olah. Chris Olah has been spearheading work into trying to interpret what neural networks are doing. A signature output of Chris Olah’s work is pictures of creepy dogs like this one:

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7509797c-bf2a-49b9-b10c-88f053d14b21_224x224.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7509797c-bf2a-49b9-b10c-88f053d14b21_224x224.png)

What’s significant about this picture is that it’s the answer to a question roughly like this: what image would maximise the activation of neuron #12345678 in a particular image-classifying neural network? (With some asterisks about needing to apply some maths details to the process to promote large-scale structure in the image to get nice-looking results, and with apologies to neuron #12345678, who I might have confused with another neuron.) If neuron #12345678 is maximised by something that looks like a dog, it’s a fair guess that this neuron somehow encodes, or is involved in encoding, the concept of “dog” inside the neural network.
response_rejected:
One of the foremost challenges with contemporary machine learning systems is our general inability to comprehend their internal workings. These models perform complex calculations involving large, opaque arrays of numbers, making it difficult to determine their precise actions. Are they meticulously analyzing an image to distinguish a cat from a dog, engaging in some form of abstract computation, or perhaps even contemplating future scenarios? It remains a mystery.

To gain insight into such questions, one might consult Chris Olah, a pioneer in the effort to understand the operations of neural networks. A hallmark of his research includes peculiar images, such as unusual depictions of dogs. The importance of such an image stems from its creation process: it is generated to maximally stimulate a specific neuron (for instance, neuron #12345678) within an image-recognition neural network. This process involves certain mathematical refinements to ensure a coherent visual output. If a particular neuron is most strongly activated by images resembling dogs, it is reasonable to infer that this neuron participates in representing the concept of "dog" within the network's structure.

response_accepted:
Advanced AI represents at least a technology that promises to have effects on the scale of the internet or computer revolutions, and perhaps even more likely to be more akin to the effects of the **industrial revolution** (which allowed for the automation of much _manual_ labour) and the **evolution of humans** (the last time something significantly smarter than everything that had come before appeared on the planet).

It’s easy to invent technologies that the same could be said about - a magic wish-granting box! Wow! But unlike magic wish-granting boxes, something like advanced AI, or AGI, or transformative AI, or [PASTA](https://www.cold-takes.com/transformative-ai-timelines-part-1-of-4-what-kind-of-ai/) (Process for Automating Scientific and Technical Achievement) seems to be headed our way. The smart money is on it very likely coming **this century**, and quite likely in the **first half**.

If you look at the progress in modern machine learning, and especially the past few years of progress in so-called deep learning, it is hard not to feel a sense of rushing progress. The past few years of progress, in particular the success of the transformer architecture, should update us in the direction that intelligence might be a surprisingly easy problem. What is essentially fancy iterative statistical curve-fitting with a few hacks thrown in already manages to write fluent appropriate English text in response to questions, create paintings from a description, and carry out multi-step logical deduction in natural language. **The fundamental problem that plagued AI progress for over half a century - getting fuzzy/intuitive/creative thinking into a machine, in addition to the sharp but brittle logic at which computers have long excelled - seems to have been cracked.**
response_rejected:
Advanced Artificial Intelligence is poised to be a technology with impacts at least as significant as the internet or computer revolutions. More than that, its effects might be more comparable to the Industrial Revolution, which automated extensive manual labor, or even the evolution of humans—the last instance of a vastly more intelligent entity emerging on Earth.

While one can easily conceive of fictional technologies with similar transformative potential, like a magical wish-granting device, advanced AI (also referred to as AGI, transformative AI, or PASTA) appears to be a genuine development on the horizon. Current expert opinion suggests it is highly probable within this century, possibly in the first 50 years.

Observing the advancements in modern machine learning, particularly in deep learning over recent years, gives a strong impression of accelerated progress. The achievements of architectures like the transformer suggest that developing intelligence might be a less formidable challenge than once believed. These systems, which are essentially sophisticated iterative statistical models with some clever additions, can already generate fluent and appropriate text, create art from descriptions, and perform complex logical reasoning in natural language. The core difficulty that hindered AI development for decades—integrating intuitive, creative thought processes with the precise but rigid logic that computers have always handled well—appears to have been overcome.

response_accepted: If you want usable energy, you need to use the forces between particles. The weakest force is gravity, but if you happen to be near a gigantic amount of material (e.g. the Earth) with an uneven surface that has stuff flowing down it (e.g. water in a river), we can still use it to generate power. This insight gives us hydropower, which delivers about 16% of the world's electricity. The main downside is that because of how weak gravity is, dams have to be large and environmentally disruptive to generate useful power. Moving to stronger forces, we have chemical interactions between atoms. In the form of burning fossil fuels, rearranging chemical bonds produces 66% of the world's electricity. The main downside is how weak chemical bonds are, and therefore how much matter has to be processed (i.e. burned) to produce energy. A lot of matter means a lot of waste products. Despite decades of work on possible safe waste-management strategies (e.g. carbon capture and storage), we still outrageously keep dumping over thirty billion tons of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere every year, with massive effects on the climate that will potentially last thousands of years, while also producing a long list of other harmful waste products that kill [a lot of people](https://ourworldindata.org/air-pollution) per year.
response_rejected: Generating useful energy requires harnessing inter-particle forces. Gravity, the least potent force, can be utilized near large landmasses with flowing water, like rivers, leading to hydropower which supplies around 16% of global electricity. However, gravity's weakness necessitates large, ecologically impactful dams for significant power generation. Stronger chemical forces, exploited by burning fossil fuels, account for 66% of the world's electricity. The drawback here is the low energy yield per unit of matter, meaning vast quantities must be burned, creating substantial waste. Even with research into safe disposal methods like carbon capture, over thirty billion tons of CO2 are annually released into the atmosphere, causing long-term climate damage and other lethal pollutants that result in many deaths each year.

response_accepted: Fusion is definitely a technology that we should develop. However, as J. Storrs Hall writes in _Where is my flying car?_ (my review [here](https://strataoftheworld.blogspot.com/2021/03/review-where-is-my-flying-car.html)): _"As a science fiction and technology fan, for most of my life I had been squarely in the “just you wait until we get fusion” camp. Then I was forced to compare the expected advantages fusion would bring to the ones we already had with fission. Fuel costs are already negligible. The process is already clean, with no emissions. Even though the national [US] waste repository at Yucca Mountain has been blocked by activists since it was designated in 1987 and never opened, fission produces so little waste that all our power plants have operated the entire period by basically sweeping it into the back closet."_ We have already invented a miracle clean power source. And, surprise surprise, we should really use it.
response_rejected: While fusion technology holds promise and warrants continued development, J. Storrs Hall, in _Where is my flying car?_, notes a shift in perspective. Initially a proponent of waiting for fusion, Hall realized upon comparing it with existing fission technology that fission already offers many of fusion's anticipated benefits. Fission's fuel is inexpensive, it operates cleanly without emissions, and produces minimal waste—so little, in fact, that power plants have managed waste storage on-site for decades, even with the Yucca Mountain repository remaining unopened due to opposition. The author concludes that we possess a remarkable clean energy solution in fission that we ought to be utilizing more fully.

response_accepted: In accidents, nuclear power plants can release insane amounts of radioactivity. Insane amounts of radiation are dangerous. However, the reaction to radiation risks is often out of proportion to the true risk – the Fukushima evacuations are considered excessive in hindsight, as argued in [this study](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2017/11/171120085453.htm), though you probably don't need to make a study to guess it from [this chart](https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/estimated-mortality-from-fukushima-nuclear-disaster). It is critically important to remember the above statistics on health effects, and not let yourself be biased by [vivid stories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chernobyl_(miniseries)) about horrible individual events. The fear of nuclear accidents is similar to the fear of flying rather than driving: statistically one is much safer, but one is much easier to fear because when things go wrong, it comes in more story-worthy packages. In particular: it is _not_ the case that nuclear power is safer only because accidents are rare and therefore get left out of statistics; nuclear power would be overwhelmingly safer than fossil fuels even if there were a Chernobyl going off every year. As I said above, [hydropower accidents](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_hydroelectric_power_station_failures) are more common, more deadly, and higher variance, so any argument based on disaster risk that bans nuclear would also ban hydropower.
response_rejected: Nuclear accidents can indeed release extremely high levels of radioactivity, which are inherently hazardous. Nevertheless, public and official responses to these radiation risks frequently exceed the actual danger, as exemplified by the Fukushima evacuations, now seen as overly cautious. It's vital to focus on statistical health impacts rather than being swayed by dramatic narratives of isolated incidents. The apprehension about nuclear accidents parallels the fear of air travel versus road travel: flying is statistically safer, yet driving accidents, though more frequent, are less sensational. Importantly, nuclear power's safety isn't merely due to infrequent accidents; it would remain significantly safer than fossil fuels even if Chernobyl-level events occurred annually. Moreover, hydropower incidents are more frequent, cause more fatalities, and have greater variability in impact, meaning that arguments against nuclear power based on disaster risk should logically apply to hydropower as well.

response_accepted: The world consumes about 20 000 TWh per year as electricity (about one-eight of total energy use – lots is used directly for transportation and heat). Let's compare this to making a drive across Europe that starts in Lisbon and ends in Tallinn. Each kilometre we travel represents a bit less than 5 TWh of energy towards our 20 000 TWh goal. Let's say walking is wind/solar/geothermal, biking is hydropower, flying is nuclear, and driving is fossil fuels. First we walk some 450 km, ending north-west of Madrid, and then bike 650 km, just barely taking us into France. We're a bit careless and somehow we've manage to shove a hundred people off wind turbines along the way. Oops. By this point we're getting tired of walking and biking, but thankfully there's a flight to Paris. The pilot has a bad day and lands on top of a crowd, flattening another hundred people. We really hate flying, so we refuse all the other offers that the airline companies try to sell us. Instead we step out of the Paris airport, rent a car, and start carelessly careening down the remaining 2600 km.
response_rejected: Global electricity consumption is approximately 20,000 TWh annually, a fraction of total energy usage which also includes direct transport and heating. To illustrate the relative risks of energy sources, imagine a journey from Lisbon to Tallinn where each kilometer equates to nearly 5 TWh of energy. In this analogy, wind, solar, and geothermal power are like walking; hydropower is biking; nuclear power is flying; and fossil fuels are driving. The journey begins with about 450 km of walking (renewables), followed by 650 km of biking (hydropower), during which, metaphorically, a hundred fatalities occur due to accidents with wind turbines. After this, a flight (nuclear power) to Paris results in another hundred deaths due to a hypothetical crash. Despite this, because of a disproportionate fear of flying, the rest of the 2600 km journey is undertaken by car (fossil fuels).

response_accepted: Nuclear waste is awkward to deal with, but it's far from the worst sort of industrial waste we deal with – consider the over thirty billion tons of carbon dioxide we've dumped into the atmosphere over the past year, or the various horrible things that coal plants spew out that cause dozens of Chernobyl-equivalents per year. Nuclear waste is not some miracle substance that effortlessly seeps everywhere and kills whatever it touches. Until 1993, countries (mostly the USSR and UK), were dumping nuclear waste into the ocean. This is rightly banned these days, but you can observe that we still have oceans; in fact, the [the environmental impacts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ocean_disposal_of_radioactive_waste#Environmental_impact) have so far been negligible except for somewhat higher concentrations of some nasty isotopes exactly at the site. In general, nuclear waste is a serious problem that has to be solved somehow, but solutions exist (currently, Finland's [Onkalo repository](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Onkalo_spent_nuclear_fuel_repository) is the closest to being operational). Though the timescale is long, it is not different in principle from some existing disposal methods for nasty things like mercury and arsenic. Is it responsible to leave behind dangerous waste for future generations? It's far more responsible than leaving them with the almost astronomical amounts of CO2 emissions that a single kilogram of uranium prevents.
response_rejected: Managing nuclear waste presents challenges, yet it's not the most problematic industrial byproduct compared to, for instance, the annual thirty billion tons of atmospheric CO2 or the harmful emissions from coal plants, which cause yearly health impacts equivalent to many Chernobyls. It's a misconception that nuclear waste is an uncontrollably pervasive and deadly material. Historically, ocean dumping of nuclear waste occurred until 1993, and while now prohibited, the environmental consequences have been minimal, mostly confined to localized isotope concentrations. Although nuclear waste is a significant issue requiring resolution, viable solutions like Finland's Onkalo repository are emerging. The long-term nature of this waste is comparable to managing other hazardous materials like mercury. When considering intergenerational responsibility, leaving behind manageable nuclear waste is preferable to burdening future generations with the massive CO2 emissions that nuclear power helps to avoid.
